coredata.dc:title: Study on oxidation and pyrolysis of carbonate esters using a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile. Part II: Chemical kinetic modeling of ethyl methyl carbonate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of The Combustion Institute.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of The Combustion Institute.
coredata.dc:creator: Takahashi, Shintaro
Kanayama, Keisuke
Morikura, Shota
Nakamura, Hisashi
Tezuka, Takuya
Maruta, Kaoru
coredata.dc:description: Little is known on reactivity of ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), which is widely used as an electrolyte solvent in lithium-ion batteries (LIB). A better understanding on the reactivity of EMC and its chemical kinetics are required to assess the fire risks of LIB. The reactivity of a stoichiometric EMC/air mixture was investigated with a weak flame in a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) at atmospheric pressure and a maximum wall temperature (T w,max) of 1300 K. To investigate species evolution formed in EMC oxidation (equivalence ratios of 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5) and pyrolysis, species measurements were performed in MFR at atmospheric pressure and T w,max range of 700–1300 K. These experimental insights were used for modeling and validation of a chemical kinetic mechanism for EMC. The first chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC was developed based on reactions of dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), and literature data. The present EMC mechanism reproduced measured species profiles well. Both experiments and computations showed two-stage increases in CO2 mole fraction: initial fuel decomposition producing CO2 at T w,max = 850 K and subsequent CO oxidation to CO2 at T w,max = 1050 K. In the first CO2 increase region, a series of decomposition reaction of EMC produced CO2, C2H4 and CH3OH via methyl formic acid. The oxidation of CH3OH and C2H4 proceeded in the region corresponding to second CO2 increase. The present EMC mechanism also reproduced experimental weak flame position of EMC well. Computational weak flame structure of EMC indicated a three-stage reaction: EMC decomposition, oxidation of decomposition products to CO and oxidation of CO to CO2. The three-stage reaction initiated by fuel decomposition reaction is distinct from the one initiated by low-temperature oxidation of ordinary hydrocarbons. This novel three-stage reaction was also observed for DEC, which has two ethyl groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microcombustion
Molecular elimination
Fire safety
Oxygenated fuel
Methoxy formic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental weak flame images of stoichiometric (a) DMC/air, (b) EMC/air and (c) DEC/air mixtures at the inlet mean flow velocity of 2 cm/s, T w,max = 1300 K and atmospheric pressure [21]. The temperatures described on the right side indicate weak flame positions of each carbonate ester.
Schematic of experimental setup of (a) speciation and (b) weak flame observation.
Molecule structures of three carbonate esters with labels of H-abstraction sites.

Rate constants of R1 (CCOC*OOC = C2H4 + COC*OOH). Black line: this study; Blue line: theoretical estimation with MP2/6–311++G(2d, p)//MP2/6–31G(d) by Notario et al. [25]; Green line: theoretical estimation with B3LYP/6–31G* by Anez et al. [26]; orange line: theoretical estimation with B3LYP/6–31+G** by Anez et al. [26]; Sky blue line: theoretical estimation with a static reaction rate rule by Chuchani et al. [27]; Pink points: measured by Gordon et al. [22]; Violet points: measured by Cross et al. [23]; and Red points: measured by Taylor et al. [24].
Measured (symbols) and computational (lines) species profiles for EMC oxidation in MFR at 1.5%EMC/O2/N2 and atmospheric pressure, for three equivalence ratios (a:ϕ = 0.5, b: ϕ = 1.0 and c: ϕ = 1.5).
Measured (symbols) and computational (lines) species profiles for EMC pyrolysis in MFR at 1.5%EMC/N2 and atmospheric pressure.

Reaction pathways for EMC oxidation in MFR at atmospheric pressure, ϕ = 1.0 and T w,max = 850 and 1050 K. Numbers in the bracket () represent the percentage of flux that goes through a particular species. Reaction numbers, R#, are the same as those described in Table 1.

Weak flame image, normalized luminosity profile and computational HRR and species profiles for a stoichiometric EMC/air mixture at atmospheric pressure in MFR. The present mechanism was used for computation.
Rate constant expression of key EMC reactions (k = AT nexp(–Ea /RT), units: cal-mol-s).

entry.italic: Unimolecular decomposition of methoxy formic acid (MFA):

row.entry: Analogy with C2H4OC2H5 = C2H5O + C2H4 from [39]
Analogy with CH3CH2COOCH2 = C2H5CO + CH2O from [40]

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: k = AT nexp(-Ea/RT): The units are in cal-mol-s. “ * ” indicates double bond. “ j ” also indicates radical site on the preceding carbon atom.

head.ce:title: Study on oxidation and pyrolysis of carbonate esters using a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile. Part II: Chemical kinetic modeling of ethyl methyl carbonate

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cResearch Alliance Center for Mathematical Science, Tohoku University, 6-3, Aoba, Aramaki, Aoba-ku, Sendai, Miyagi 980-8578 Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Little is known on reactivity of ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), which is widely used as an electrolyte solvent in lithium-ion batteries (LIB). A better understanding on the reactivity of EMC and its chemical kinetics are required to assess the fire risks of LIB. The reactivity of a stoichiometric EMC/air mixture was investigated with a weak flame in a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) at atmospheric pressure and a maximum wall temperature (T w,max) of 1300 K. To investigate species evolution formed in EMC oxidation (equivalence ratios of 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5) and pyrolysis, species measurements were performed in MFR at atmospheric pressure and T w,max range of 700–1300 K. These experimental insights were used for modeling and validation of a chemical kinetic mechanism for EMC. The first chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC was developed based on reactions of dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), and literature data.

The present EMC mechanism reproduced measured species profiles well. Both experiments and computations showed two-stage increases in CO2 mole fraction: initial fuel decomposition producing CO2 at T w,max = 850 K and subsequent CO oxidation to CO2 at T w,max = 1050 K. In the first CO2 increase region, a series of decomposition reaction of EMC produced CO2, C2H4 and CH3OH via methyl formic acid. The oxidation of CH3OH and C2H4 proceeded in the region corresponding to second CO2 increase. The present EMC mechanism also reproduced experimental weak flame position of EMC well. Computational weak flame structure of EMC indicated a three-stage reaction: EMC decomposition, oxidation of decomposition products to CO and oxidation of CO to CO2. The three-stage reaction initiated by fuel decomposition reaction is distinct from the one initiated by low-temperature oxidation of ordinary hydrocarbons. This novel three-stage reaction was also observed for DEC, which has two ethyl groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) become increasingly important because of their high energy density. However, as the demand for LIBs increases, many cases of mechanical shock, thermal shock, short-circuits, manufacturing defect and battery aging leading to ignition have been reported [1,2]. Carbonate esters, which are flammable substances, are major components used in the electrolytic solvent of LIBs [3–5]. Harris et al. [6] clarified fundamental investigation on combustion chemistry of carbonate esters used in LIBs. From the viewpoint of fire safety of LIBs, understanding on reactivity of carbonate esters and their chemical kinetics are essential.

Dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) are also considered as attractive biofuels that can be used to displace petroleum-derived diesel [7]. Adding DMC or DEC to diesel has an advantage to a reduction in the emission of particulate matter (PM) because of high oxygen contents of DMC and DEC. Kozak et al. [8] reported that the particulate emissions from a diesel engine are reduced when DEC is mixed with diesel. Zhang et al. [9] reported that diesel with DMC reduced PM emissions without affecting NOx emissions. For the use as additives in diesel engines, the combustion characteristics of carbonate esters have been well studied for DMC [10–17] and DEC [18–20]. For instance, Sinha and Thomson [10] reported the chemical structure of opposed flow diffusion flame of DMC for the first time. Detailed chemical kinetic mechanisms for DMC [15–17] and DEC [19,20] have been developed. The detailed chemical kinetic mechanism of DMC was first published by Glaude et al. [11] based on a mechanism of dimethoxy methyl (DMM) and DME, then followed by Hu et al. [15], Sun et al. [17] and Alexandrino et al. [16]. See previous work (Part I) [21] about major differences between the four DMC mechanisms. That of DEC was first developed by Nakamura et al. [19] followed by Sun et al. [20]. On the other hand, there are no studies on combustion properties of EMC to the authors’ knowledge. Only a few studies on EMC pyrolysis were conducted. Gordon and Norris [22], Cross et al. [23] and Tayler [24] obtained experimental decomposition rates of EMC at around 600 K along with other carbonates using a reaction vessel. The decomposition rate of EMC has been investigated in theoretical studies from Notario et al. [25], Anez et al. [26] and Chuchani et al. [27]. However, no chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC has been reported before.

In this study, we investigate the oxidation and pyrolysis of EMC using a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) [28], which allows us to study oxidation/pyrolysis of sample along the temperature profile. MFR consists of a reactor whose inner diameter is smaller than a conventional quenching diameter and an external heat source. A stationary temperature profile is formed on the tube inner wall surface along the tube axis by external heating. At very low inlet velocities of a few cm/s for a fuel/air mixture, a stable flame with faint chemiluminescence called weak flame is formed in the MFR [29]. Under such conditions, the gas phase temperature is strongly constrained by the given wall temperature. The mild temperature gradient in MFR also makes it possible to evaluate the oxidation of the fuel in a spatially separated manner. In our earlier studies, a three-stage oxidation of n-C7H16/air [30] and DME/air mixture [31] including low-temperature oxidation was observed. The three-stage oxidation corresponds to cool flame, blue flame and hot flame, respectively, from the low-temperature side. A theoretical analysis of Minaev et al. [32] indicated that the weak flame represents the ignition phenomena because its position is located on the ignition branch of the modified Fendell carve [33]. By comparing weak flame positions, the reactivity of fuels can be investigated. In our earlier studies, the comparisons of reactivity among C1—C4 alkanes [34], gasoline surrogates (n-C7H16/isoC8H18 mixtures) with various RON [35] and even low-reactivity samples such as hydrofluorocarbons [36,37] were investigated by weak flame positions.

In the study of carbonate esters using a weak flame in MFR, the reactivity of DMC, DEC and EMC was evaluated in a previous paper (Part I) [21]. Figure 1 shows the weak flame images of carbonate esters: (a) DMC/air, (b) EMC/air and (c) DEC/air mixtures. The reactivity of DEC and EMC is higher than that of DMC, which is attributed to the fact that the fuel decomposition reaction involving the ethyl group of DEC occurs at low temperature. This trend suggests that the fuel decomposition is likely to be dominant in EMC as well in DEC at initial oxidation stage, resulting on higher reactivity of EMC than DMC. This finding will be highly suggestive in later part of chemical kinetic modeling of EMC.

In this study, a chemical kinetic mechanism for EMC was developed. Composition profiles from the MFR over a wide temperature range and wide equivalence ratios and reactivity evaluation of weak flame were obtained. The set of experimental data was used for the validation of the developed chemical kinetic mechanism. The final objective of a series of studies including the previous paper (Part I) [21] is to construct a gas-phase chemical kinetic mechanism for substance used in LIB electrolyte solvents to prevent fires. As a first step, the chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC in addition to DMC and DEC is reported.

The experimental setup and computational method for speciation and weak flame observation were same as the methods on the earlier study [38] and the previous study Part I [21]. In this section, a brief introduction of the experimental apparatus and computational methods is given.

Figure 2 (a) depicts a schematic of experimental setup. A quartz tube with an inner diameter of 2 mm was used as a reactor channel. EMC/O2/N2 mixtures were supplied from a heated tank to the reactor at a mean inlet flow velocity of 2 cm/s. The experimental uncertainty of the inlet mixture composition that includes mixing process in the tank and controlling of flow rate is estimated to be approximately ± 5%. High-purity reactants were used respectively; oxygen (> 99.9999% pure), nitrogen (> 99.999% pure) and EMC (EMC anhydrous, > 99.5% pure from Kishida Chemical Co., Ltd.). The equivalence ratio (ϕ) was varied at 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and ∞ (pyrolysis) by controlling flow rates of fuel, O2 and N2 with mass flow controllers. The fuel fraction was set to 1.5% at all equivalence ratio conditions. Experiments were conducted at atmospheric pressure. For species measurement, an electric heater was attached to the reactor as the heat source to provide the inner wall surface with stationary temperature profiles. The temperature profile given by the electric heater is more stable than the one given by a hydrogen/air flat-flame burner. The speciation data were obtained by varying the maximum wall temperature of the reactor between T w,max = 700–1300 K by every 50 or 100 K (11 temperature profiles in total, see supplementary material). The temperature profile of the inner wall surface of the reactor was measured with a K-type thermocouple inserted from the downstream of the reactor. Exhaust gas was directly introduced into a sampler unit from the exit of MFR through a sampling line. Sampled gas was then analyzed by GC (Shimadzu Tracera) with a barrier discharge ionization detector (BID). O2, H2, CO, CO2, CH4, C2H2 and C2H4 were measured by GC. Experiments were repeated at least three times for each condition and uncertainties in measured mole fractions were shown as error bars. The uncertainty of T w in the weak flame observation is ± 5 K and that in the species measurement is approximately ± 3%.

The reactivity evaluation of EMC was conducted by comparing the experimental and computational weak flame positions. Fig. 2(b) shows a schematic of the experimental setup of weak flame observation. One difference in the setup is that a hydrogen/air flat-flame burner was employed as an external heat source instead of the electric heater for the weak flame observation. A stationary temperature profile with T w max = 1300 K was formed on the inner wall surface of the reactor. A stoichiometric EMC/O2/N2 mixture (79:21 in N2:O2) was supplied to the reactor at the inlet velocity of 2 cm/s at atmospheric pressure. A digital still camera (Nikon D800) with a CH band-pass filter (transparent wavelength: 431.2 nm, half bandwidth: 6.4 nm) was used to observe weak flames at an exposure time of 30 s. Experimental weak flame position was defined as a maximum peak location in the tube-axis direction of the luminosity profile that was averaged in the tube-radial direction. The uncertainty of T w at weak flame position is approximately ± 5 K.

To validate the chemical kinetic mechanism and to conduct an analysis of the production of measured species, one-dimensional steady-state computations were conducted using PREMIX code in ANSYS Chemkin-Pro-v19.0 with an additional term of convective heat transfer between the gas and the reactor wall in a gas-phase energy equation [28]. Computational conditions (mixture compositions, inlet flow velocity, wall temperature profile and pressure) were the same as those in the experiments. The wall temperature profile used in the computations is available in the supplementary material. The computational domain was set to 0–10 cm. Species mole fractions at x = 10 cm were compared with experimental results. A detailed chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC was developed in this study and was used in the computations.

The detailed chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC was developed based on the DEC mechanism of Nakamura et al. [19] because the DEC mechanism reproduced well measured species profile and weak flame position of DEC in the previous paper (Part I) [21]. The considered EMC reactions in this study were added in the DEC mechanism. For constructing a comprehensive kinetic mechanism of carbonate esters, the DMC reactions from the DMC mechanism of Alexandrino et al. [16] were also added in the developed mechanism. Alexandrino mechanism was chosen because it predicted well measured species profile and weak flame position of DMC in the previous paper (Part I) [21]. The updates of reactions in this study have little effect on combustion properties of DMC and DEC (See Fig. S2 and S3 in supplementary material).

The molecular structures of DMC, EMC and DEC are shown in Fig. 3 . The key reactions for EMC are shown in Table 1 and discussed below. All reactions added for EMC were also described in the supplementary material (See Table S1).

The unimolecular decomposition of EMC via molecular elimination, i.e. retroene reaction, is the most important fuel decomposition due to its low activation energy. The decomposition of EMC (R1 in Table 1) produces C2H4 and methoxy formic acid (MFA). As summarized in Fig. 4 , rate constants of R1 were obtained from the measurement of Gordon and Norris [22], Cross et al. [23] and Taylor [24] and from the theoretical calculations of Notario et al. [25] with MP2/6–311++G(2d, p)//MP2/6–31G(d) level, Anez et al. [26] with B3LYP/6–31G* or B3LYP/6–31+G** levels and Chuchani et al. [27] using a static reaction rate rule among 2-substituted ethyl methyl carbonates. Notario et al. [25] suggested that two kind of rate constants for R1. One is blue line in Fig. 4 and another is black line. The latter R1 is 1/3 of the former. They recommended the latter one that was better fit to the experimental rate constants of Cross et al. [23] and Taylor [24] within the estimated uncertainty limit. In the present study, we used the rate constant indicated by black line in Fig. 4. The theoretical analysis [25–27] showed that EMC mainly decomposes into C2H4 and MFA as it proceeds through a 6-membered ring transition state (TS) including an ethyl ester group. Notario et al. and Anez et al. found that DEC decomposes into C2H5OH and C2H4, which is the same as the decomposition of EMC ethyl side. The rate constant of the DEC decomposition proposed by Notario et al. [25] is approximately 2.7 times higher than that of R1. The higher rate constant of the DEC decomposition than that of R1 is attributed to two ethyl groups of DEC. The MFA produced from R1 then undergoes rapid decomposition to produce CH3OH and CO2 (R4 in Table 1), which occurs via a 4-membered ring TS. The rate constant of R4 was taken from Notario et al. [25]. CH3OH reactions were the same as those employed in the base mechanism. These two reactions (R1 and R4) lead to the fuel decomposition of EMC, producing C2H4, CH3OH and CO2, which is the main consumption path of EMC.

The direct decomposition of EMC to CH3OH, CO2 and C2H4 (not via MFA) was considered as R2. R2 proceeds via 6-membered ring TS involving β-site H atom in the ethyl group and C = O double bonds. Nucleophilic substitution reactions (R3) giving CO2 and ether (ethyl methyl ether, EME) were also considered from the literature [25]. These two reactions have higher energy barriers than R1 and are therefore minor consumption pathways of EMC. This study applies the EME mechanism taken from the work of Yasunaga et al. [39].
The rate constants of the H-atom abstraction reactions from EMC were determined by analogy with those from DMC for the methyl side of EMC and from DEC for the ethyl side of EMC in this study.

In DMC mechanism [16], the rate constants of H-atom abstraction reactions from the DMC methyl group with H and O radicals are taken from the theoretical work of Peukert et al. [41]. The H-abstraction reactions with other radicals (OH, HO2, CH3, CH3O2, CH3O, C2H3 and C2H5) are estimated on the analogy from methyl butanoate by Dooley et al. [42]. Because EMC has one methyl group, the rate constant of the H-atom abstraction reaction from the EMC methyl group was adopted with that from DMC by multiplying 0.5 to the A-factors in the present mechanism.

In DEC mechanism [19], the rate constants of the H-atom abstraction reactions from the α- and β-sites with OH and HO2 radicals are analogous to those of Mendez et al. [43,44] for ethyl ester. For the H-abstraction reactions from DEC with other radicals (H, O, CH3, CH3O, CH3O2, C2H3 and C2H5), the rate constants are built on the analogy of H-abstraction reactions from alkanes. DEC has two methylene groups at the α-site and each group is linked to an oxygen atom (See Fig. 3), i.e., CH bonds of the methylene groups in DEC are weaker than those of a methylene group in an alkane. The DEC mechanism uses the rate constant of a weaker tertiary CH bond in an alkane for H-atom abstraction reactions from the DEC α-site. Because EMC has one ethyl group, the rate constants of the H-atom abstraction reactions from the EMC ethyl group were adopted with those from DEC by multiplying 0.5 to the A-factors in the present mechanism.

The β-site ethyl EMC radical (CjCOC*OOC) decomposes into a radical (COC*OOj) and C2H4 (R5). The authors addressed the reaction (R5) with the same rate as a CH3O radical adding to C2H4 [45]. Further investigation is needed in this reaction type which also controls the decomposition of ester radicals. The decomposition of COC*OOj gives CH3O and CO2 (R9), whose rate constant is taken from the DMC mechanism [16]. The isomerization reaction of COC*OOj radical to CjOC*OOH radical, however, is not considered in this study because the reaction can only take a 5-membered ring structure and the rate constant of the isomerization reaction with 5-membered ring generally has high energy barrier. It can be justified by the fact that the activation energy for isomerization reaction of CCOC*OOj radical has 6.71 kcal/mol for that via 5-membered ring and 2.69 kcal/mol for that via 6-membered ring [19]. The isomerization reaction via the 6-membered ring is 100 times faster at 800 K than that via the 5-membered ring.

For reaction R6, the α-site ethyl EMC radical (CCjOC*OOC) decomposes to methoxy carbonyl radical (COCj*O) and acetaldehyde (CH3CHO). The authors chose the rate constant from an analogous reaction involving formaldehyde (CH2O) based on methyl propanoate (CCC*OOC) [40]. Methoxy carbonyl radical (COCj*O) is formed from R6 and rate constants for their decomposition reactions (R12 and R13) are taken from the DMC mechanism [16].

The decomposition of methyl EMC radical (CCOC*OOCj) has two pathways: Ethoxy carbonyl radical (CCOCj*O) and formaldehyde (CH2O) (R7); COCj*O radical and acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) (R8). CCOCj*O further decomposes into C2H5O and CO (R10) or C2H5 and CO2 (R11). The series of decomposition reactions related to methyl EMC radical (R7, R8, R10 and R11) were taken from those in the DEC mechanism [19]. Note that R7 expresses the decomposition as a reverse reaction.

The thermodynamic parameters for the species are essential because they are used to determine reverse rate constants and determine the heat release rate per reaction. The thermodynamic and transport parameters for EMC-related species (CCOC*OOC, CjCOC*OOC, CCjOC*OOC, COC*OOj, COCj*O) were taken from those in the DEC mechanism [19]. The THERM (THermo Estimation for Radicals and Molecules) [46] software was used to calculate the thermochemical properties of MFA (COC*OOH), which is one of the most important intermediates in the initial stage of EMC decomposition. The THERM group values were taken by KUCRS (Knowledge-basing Utilities for Complex Reaction Systems) program [47]. The transport parameters of COC*OOH were taken from those of CjOC*OOH in the DEC mechanism [19]. The transport parameters of radical species (COCj*O) were taken from the DMC mechanism [16]. The developed mechanism in CHEMKIN format is available in supplementary material. In addition to EMC, the present mechanism includes DMC and DEC. The total number of species is 371 and that of reactions is 2076.

A comparison between experimental and computational species profiles in MFR at 1.5%EMC/O2/N2 mixture and atmospheric pressure, for three equivalence ratios (a:ϕ =0.5, b: ϕ =1.0 and c: ϕ =1.5), as a function of the maximum wall temperature is depicted in Fig. 5 . Experimental results show the formation of C2H4 and CO2, which are products from EMC decomposition reactions, at low temperatures of 750—850 K at all equivalence ratios. On the other hand, the consumption of O2 is not observed at these temperatures. Experimental results suggest that EMC decomposition proceeds in 750—850 K. The consumption of O2 and C2H4 starts at 900 K. Simultaneously, H2, CO and CH4 start to be produced. For ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0, H2, CO and CH4 start to be consumed and CO2 start to be further produced at 1000 K. Two-stage CO2 increase was observed. As a result, multi-stage reaction of EMC appeared, i.e., production of C2H4 and CO2 from EMC decomposition at 750–850 K, oxidation of decomposition products to CO at 900–1000 K and oxidation of CO to CO2 at 1050 K. For ϕ = 1.5, mole fractions of H2 and CO keep high and that of CH4 remains at high temperatures. Oxidation of EMC decomposition products proceeds in 900—1000 K. Two-staged CO2 increases at 750—850 K and 1000 K were also observed for DEC in the previous study [21]. The present mechanism satisfactorily reproduced the measured results. Note that a small amount of C2H2 is observed in the ϕ = 1.5 case at high temperatures as observed in supplementary material (Fig. S4).

To discuss the EMC decomposition at low temperatures, experimental and computational results in the pyrolysis condition are depicted in Fig. 6 . To avoid soot formation at high temperatures, measurements were made below T w,max = 1000 K. At T w,max=750—850 K, the formation of CO2 and C2H4 is observed. This temperature range agrees with the temperature range where the formation of CO2 and C2H4 is observed in the oxidation condition. The temperature range of the EMC decomposition also agrees with that of the DEC decomposition [21].

Figure 7 depicts the major reaction pathway of stoichiometric EMC oxidation at T w,max = 850 and 1050 K. Bold style in species names indicates measured species. At 850 K, the decomposition reaction of R1 is dominant and produces MFA (COC*OOH) and C2H4. Other decomposition reactions of EMC (R2 and R3) have little contribution compared to R1. The rapid decomposition of MFA (R4) produces CH3OH and CO2. Because of low temperature, reactions of CH3OH and C2H4 do not proceed. On the other hand, these reactions proceed at 1050 K. CH3OH oxidation toward CO and CO2 proceeds via C1 pathway. C2H4 mainly forms C2 species and CH3 radical. Compared to the oxidation of CH3OH and C2H4, EMC decomposition reaction is minor at 1050 K.

Figure 8 portrays a weak flame image, normalized luminosity profile, computational HRR and species profiles for a stoichiometric EMC/air mixture at atmospheric pressure. The weak flame image and luminosity profile show a single luminous zone. The luminosity peak is located at the wall temperature of 1128 K (x = 5.10 cm). The present mechanism has the HRR peak at 1120 K (x = 5.07 cm), which agrees with the experimental weak flame position.

The HRR profile shows negative values around 900 K (x = 4.5 cm). In this negative HRR region, EMC is fully consumed while O2 is partially consumed. C2H4, CH3OH and CO2 are formed in the negative HRR zone. The negative HRR results from EMC decomposition reaction. In x = 4.6–5.0 cm, C2H4 and CH3OH are oxidized to produce CO, where a plateau is formed in the HRR profile. In x = 5.0–5.3 cm, CO is consumed to produce CO2 having the HRR peak. Based on the computational profiles, three-stage reaction of EMC appeared, i.e., EMC decomposition, oxidation of decomposition products to CO and oxidation of CO to CO2, which also observed from the species measurement data in Fig. 5.

The three-stage reaction was also observed in DEC [21]. The major difference in the three-stage reaction between DEC and EMC is decomposition products: C2H5OH, C2H4 and CO2 for DEC; CH3OH, C2H4 and CO2 for EMC. In comparison with the flame structures of DEC and DMC in the previous paper [21], EMC weak flame structure is similar to that of DEC: that is, a dominant fuel decomposition reaction producing alcohol, C2H4 and CO2 in the initial stage of oxidation. As described in Section 3.1, the rate constant of the DEC decomposition reaction is 2.7 times faster than that of EMC decomposition, while the weak flame positions of EMC and DEC are almost the same. This is probably because of the difference in the produced alcohol. From flow-reactor study by Norton and Dryer [48], it was reported that the CH3OH oxidation to CO has a relatively strong heat release compared to other alcohols’ oxidation to CO. The contribution of heat release in the CO formation region, i.e., the second-stage reaction in Fig. 8, to the total heat release in EMC is larger than that in DEC (Fig. 6 in the previous paper [21]); 8.4 J/cm3/s at x = 4.9 cm for EMC and 1.4 J/cm3/s at x = 4.9 cm for DEC. In this region, the major pathway from the CH2OH radical to CO proceeds along the C1 pathway (CH2OH→CH2O→HCO→CO).

As described above, the present mechanism reproduces the weak flame position and measured species of EMC well. For other combustion properties, computational laminar flame speeds of DMC, DEC and EMC with air as a function of the equivalence ratio and initial temperature using the present mechanism are shown in the supplementary material (See Fig. S5 and S6). In Fig. S5 and S6, experimental laminar flames of DMC [12–14] and DEC [18] are also plotted. The laminar flame speeds of EMC were close to those of DEC. Further experimental data would be necessary to validate the mechanism and to obtain further insight into EMC oxidation.

Species profiles of EMC were measured using the micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) for oxidation and pyrolysis conditions at a temperature range of 700–1300 K and atmospheric pressure. Weak flame position in MFR, suggesting the reactivity of a sample, was observed for a stoichiometric EMC/air mixture at the maximum wall temperature of 1300 K and atmospheric pressure. The first chemical kinetic mechanism of EMC was developed based on reactions of dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), and literature data. The present mechanism reproduced the measured species data and weak flame position well. The weak flame structure analysis indicates a three-stage reaction: EMC decomposition, oxidation of decomposition products to CO and oxidation of CO to CO2. This three-stage reaction initiated by a fuel decomposition reaction was also observed for DEC in our previous paper [21]. However, it is different from one initiated by low-temperature oxidation observed in our earlier studies with n-C7H16/air [30] and DME/air mixtures [31].
Further updates of the present mechanism would be necessary for constructing a comprehensive chemical reaction mechanism of LIB electrolyte solvents that include salts and additives.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by JSPS KAKENHI grand number 16H06068, 19KK0372 and 20J12398.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.combustflame.2021.111878.
